summary: George Hotz argues against the AI 'hard takeoff' or FOOM scenario by distinguishing between closed games like Go and the open complexity of the real universe. While AI achieved superhuman performance in games through self-play, the universe contains computers and other players that must be modeled, making dominance impossible without majority compute. He uses the hedge fund analogy to show that predicting markets requires modeling all other market participants, including their computers. Hotz concludes that compute will naturally distribute in a power law, and the real risk comes from artificial restrictions that could create a 'powder keg' where one actor breaks the rules.
key_points: The universe has incomparably more complexity than any game - it's a difference in kind, not degree, since the universe contains the players themselves
AI dominance in games doesn't translate to reality because games don't include the computers playing them, but the real world must model all competing AI systems
The hedge fund analogy: everyone is already trying to predict markets with AI, but you'd need more compute than the entire market to dominate
Compute will naturally distribute in a power law, preventing any single system from achieving dominance
The dangerous scenario isn't unrestricted AI development - it's artificial FLOPS caps that create powder kegs where a single rule-breaker gains asymmetric advantage
money_quotes: AI looked scary. It looked like it was one FOOM away from self playing and becoming superhuman at the universe.
Compression is prediction is intelligence, intelligence is prediction is compression.
Your dynamics model of the universe must include computers. You'll have to spend a lot of effort modeling them.
The computers are active players, and they have a lot of compute. They might even be using it to try to model you!
The smart regulation isn't capping the FLOPS in training runs. That's creating a powder keg.
If you don't want FOOM, you just need to prevent a 51% attack on compute.
More intelligence leads to more new tricks, but the tricks get harder and harder to find.
The information revolution will do for intelligence what the industrial revolution did for energy.
key_insight: AI can't 'take off' and dominate the universe because unlike closed games, the real world contains other computers and players that must be modeled, ensuring compute advantages remain distributed rather than concentrated.
